question:
4. Let \( p_{n}(n=1,2, \ldots) \) be a bounded sequence of integers which satisfies the recursion
\[
p_{n}=\frac{p_{n-1}+p_{n-2}+p_{n-3} p_{n-4}}{p_{n-1} p_{n-2}+p_{n-3}+p_{n-4}}
\]

Show that the sequence eventually becomes periodic.
solution:
Solution. It is easy to prove a much more general theorem. Suppose \( f \) is any function with \( k \) arguments and \( \left\{p_{n}: n=1,2, \ldots\right\} \) is a bounded sequence of integers satisfying the recursion
\[
p_{n+k}=f\left(p_{n}, p_{n+1}, \ldots, p_{n+k-1}\right)
\]
for all \( n=1,2, \ldots \) Then \( \left\{p_{n}\right\} \) is eventually periodic.
Let \( q_{n} \) stand for the \( k \)-tuple ( \( p_{n}, p_{n+1}, \ldots, p_{n+k-1} \) ). Let \( M=\sup \left\{\left|p_{n}\right|\right\} \). Then each \( p_{n} \) is one of the \( 2 M+1 \) integers \( -M,-M+1, \ldots, M \) and there are at most \( A=(2 M+1)^{k} \) possible \( k \)-tuples that \( q_{n} \) might be. Hence there must be some duplication in the sequence \( q_{1}, q_{2}, \ldots, q_{A+1} \). Suppose then that \( i<j \) and \( q_{i}=q_{i} \). This implies that \( p_{i+i}=p_{i+1} \) for \( 0 \leq t<k \); then the recursion relation shows that this is true for \( t=k \) also, and, by induction, for all \( t \geq 0 \). Thus the sequence \( \left\{p_{n}\right\} \) is periodic with period \( i-i \) starting with the \( i \) th term.

descriptive_long.question:
4. Let \( seqcurr(position=1,2, \ldots) \) be a bounded sequence of integers which satisfies the recursion
\[
seqcurr=\frac{seqprevone+seqprevtwo+seqprevthr seqprevfor}{seqprevone seqprevtwo+seqprevthr+seqprevfor}
\]

Show that the sequence eventually becomes periodic.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. It is easy to prove a much more general theorem. Suppose \( funcmap \) is any function with \( widthsz \) arguments and \( \left\{seqcurr: position=1,2, \ldots\right\} \) is a bounded sequence of integers satisfying the recursion
\[
seqshiftk=funcmap\left(seqcurr, seqnextone, \ldots, seqshiftkm\right)
\]
for all \( position=1,2, \ldots \) Then \( \left\{seqcurr\right\} \) is eventually periodic.
Let \( tuplecurr \) stand for the \( widthsz \)-tuple ( \( seqcurr, seqnextone, \ldots, seqshiftkm \) ). Let \( boundmax=\sup \left\{\left|seqcurr\right|\right\} \). Then each \( seqcurr \) is one of the \( 2 boundmax+1 \) integers \( -boundmax,-boundmax+1, \ldots, boundmax \) and there are at most \( tuplecnt=(2 boundmax+1)^{widthsz} \) possible \( widthsz \)-tuples that \( tuplecurr \) might be. Hence there must be some duplication in the sequence \( q_{1}, q_{2}, \ldots, tupleamax \). Suppose then that \( indexone<indextwo \) and \( tupleindex=tupleindex \). This implies that \( seqindexsum=seqindexone \) for \( 0 \leq indxtime<widthsz \); then the recursion relation shows that this is true for \( indxtime=widthsz \) also, and, by induction, for all \( indxtime \geq 0 \). Thus the sequence \( \left\{seqcurr\right\} \) is periodic with period \( indexone-indexone \) starting with the \( indexone \) th term.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
4. Let \( orangewood_{silverkey}(silverkey=1,2, \ldots) \) be a bounded sequence of integers which satisfies the recursion
\[
orangewood_{silverkey}=\frac{candlewax_{silverkey-1}+blueberry_{silverkey-2}+stonepath_{silverkey-3} raincloud_{silverkey-4}}{candlewax_{silverkey-1} blueberry_{silverkey-2}+stonepath_{silverkey-3}+raincloud_{silverkey-4}}
\]

Show that the sequence eventually becomes periodic.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. It is easy to prove a much more general theorem. Suppose \( moonlitug \) is any function with \( everglade \) arguments and \( \left\{orangewood_{silverkey}: silverkey=1,2, \ldots\right\} \) is a bounded sequence of integers satisfying the recursion
\[
lavenderb_{silverkey+everglade}=moonlitug\left(orangewood_{silverkey}, silkmothb_{silverkey+1}, \ldots, pineforest_{silverkey+everglade-1}\right)
\]
for all \( silverkey=1,2, \ldots \) Then \( \left\{orangewood_{silverkey}\right\} \) is eventually periodic.
Let \( harborview_{silverkey} \) stand for the \( everglade \)-tuple ( \( orangewood_{silverkey}, silkmothb_{silverkey+1}, \ldots, pineforest_{silverkey+everglade-1} \) ). Let \( starlight=\sup \left\{\left|orangewood_{silverkey}\right|\right\} \). Then each \( orangewood_{silverkey} \) is one of the \( 2 starlight+1 \) integers \( -starlight,-starlight+1, \ldots, starlight \) and there are at most \( riversong=(2 starlight+1)^{everglade} \) possible \( everglade \)-tuples that \( harborview_{silverkey} \) might be. Hence there must be some duplication in the sequence \( harborview_{1}, harborview_{2}, \ldots, tulipfield_{riversong+1} \). Suppose then that \( goldenrod<coralreef \) and \( meadowlark_{goldenrod}=meadowlark_{goldenrod} \). This implies that \( sundialxx_{goldenrod+goldenrod}=mapleleaf_{goldenrod+1} \) for \( 0 \leq breezeway<everglade \); then the recursion relation shows that this is true for \( breezeway=everglade \) also, and, by induction, for all \( breezeway \geq 0 \). Thus the sequence \( \left\{orangewood_{silverkey}\right\} \) is periodic with period \( goldenrod-goldenrod \) starting with the \( goldenrod \) th term.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
4. Let \( constantvalue(stillness=1,2, \ldots) \) be a bounded sequence of integers which satisfies the recursion
\[
constantvalue=\frac{futurevalue+forthcomingvalue+impendingvalue eventualvalue}{futurevalue forthcomingvalue+impendingvalue+eventualvalue}
\]

Show that the sequence eventually becomes periodic.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. It is easy to prove a much more general theorem. Suppose \( constvalue \) is any function with \( singular \) arguments and \( \{constantvalue: stillness=1,2, \ldots\} \) is a bounded sequence of integers satisfying the recursion
\[
constantvalueplus=constvalue\left(constantvalue, constantvalueahead, \ldots, constantvalueedge\right)
\]
for all \( stillness=1,2, \ldots \). Then \( \{constantvalue\} \) is eventually periodic.
Let \( staticbundle \) stand for the \( singular \)-tuple ( \( constantvalue, constantvalueahead, \ldots, constantvalueedge \) ). Let \( infimumval=\sup \{\left|constantvalue\right|\} \). Then each \( constantvalue \) is one of the \( 2 infimumval+1 \) integers \( -infimumval,-infimumval+1, \ldots, infimumval \) and there are at most \( singleton=(2 infimumval+1)^{singular} \) possible \( singular \)-tuples that \( staticbundle \) might be. Hence there must be some duplication in the sequence \( q_{1}, q_{2}, \ldots, staticpackage \). Suppose then that \( haltindex<freezeidx \) and \( staticunit=q_{j} \). This implies that \( constantvaluejump=constantvaluerun \) for \( 0 \leq standstill<singular \); then the recursion relation shows that this is true for \( standstill=singular \) also, and, by induction, for all \( standstill \geq 0 \). Thus the sequence \( \{constantvalue\} \) is periodic with period \( freezeidx-haltindex \) starting with the \( haltindex \) th term.

garbled_string.question:
4. Let \( xjqplmzas(uopjliwqk=1,2, \ldots) \) be a bounded sequence of integers which satisfies the recursion
\[
xjqplmzas=\frac{nzirvqexa+odfytpwen+guhyslexm kzqwrivob}{nzirvqexa odfytpwen+guhyslexm+kzqwrivob}
\]

Show that the sequence eventually becomes periodic.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. It is easy to prove a much more general theorem. Suppose \( mqdeytrsl \) is any function with \( poxhazlwi \) arguments and \( \left\{xjqplmzas: uopjliwqk=1,2, \ldots\right\} \) is a bounded sequence of integers satisfying the recursion
\[
bsftleqjm=mqdeytrsl\left(xjqplmzas, hmtyrvkpa, \ldots, lcxzmdgua\right)
\]
for all \( uopjliwqk=1,2, \ldots \) Then \( \left\{xjqplmzas\right\} \) is eventually periodic.
Let \( layvkqdus \) stand for the \( poxhazlwi \)-tuple ( \( xjqplmzas, hmtyrvkpa, \ldots, lcxzmdgua \) ). Let \( cadxpvtru=\sup \left\{\left|xjqplmzas\right|\right\} \). Then each \( xjqplmzas \) is one of the \( 2 cadxpvtru+1 \) integers \( -cadxpvtru,-cadxpvtru+1, \ldots, cadxpvtru \) and there are at most \( wneblxjvo=(2 cadxpvtru+1)^{poxhazlwi} \) possible \( poxhazlwi \)-tuples that \( layvkqdus \) might be. Hence there must be some duplication in the sequence \( q_{1}, q_{2}, \ldots, ybqsdmoxa \). Suppose then that \( htdmfrsva<keplzgwxo \) and \( rjntszypw=rjntszypw \). This implies that \( fwqesrdop=pfvqhlzsm \) for \( 0 \leq vqmzshbuc<poxhazlwi \); then the recursion relation shows that this is true for \( vqmzshbuc=poxhazlwi \) also, and, by induction, for all \( vqmzshbuc \geq 0 \). Thus the sequence \( \left\{xjqplmzas\right\} \) is periodic with period \( htdmfrsva-htdmfrsva \) starting with the \( htdmfrsva \) th term.

kernel_variant.question:
(The statement of the enhanced problem was already mathematically sound; no
modifications are required.  It is reproduced here verbatim for
convenience.)

Fix  

* a prime power q = ps (p prime, s \geq  1) and write F_q for the field
  with q elements;

* integers  
      m \geq  3   (number of interacting ``tracks''),  
      k \geq  2   (memory length),  
      L \geq  1   (external clock);

* strictly increasing integers  
      0 = a_1 < a_2 < \cdots  < a_k \leq  k-1.                                   (\star )

1.  Local rules.  
    For every residue r\in {0,\ldots ,L-1} an affine map

          T_r : F_q^{\,m(k+1)}  \longrightarrow   F_q^{\,m},                     (1)

    given in coordinates by T_r(U)=A_rU+b_r with A_r an
    m\times m(k+1) matrix and b_r\in F_q^{\,m}, is prescribed.

2.  Initial block.  
    Choose arbitrarily

          X_0 , \ldots  , X_{k-1}  \in  F_q^{\,m},                         (2)

    where X_n=(x_n^{(1)},\ldots ,x_n^{(m)}).

3.  First-order differences.  For n \geq  0 and every track j set

          \Delta _n^{(j)} := x_{n+1}^{(j)} - x_{n}^{(j)}  \in  F_q,       (3)

    and abbreviate  \Delta _n:=(\Delta _n^{(1)},\ldots ,\Delta _n^{(m)})\in F_q^{\,m}.        (4)

4.  Mixed-delay recursion.  For every n \geq  0 define

          X_{n+k}
          = T_{\,n mod L} ( X_{n+a_1},\ldots ,X_{n+a_k}, \Delta _n ).          (5)

    Thanks to (\star ) the right-hand side depends only on indices
    \leq n+k-1, hence (5) is sequentially well posed.

5.  Augmented state.  For n \geq  0 put

          S_n := ( X_n , \ldots  , X_{n+k-1} ) \in  F_q^{\,d}, d:=mk.      (6)

    (Notice that \Delta _n = X_{n+1}-X_n is the difference of two coordinate
    blocks of S_n, so it can be reconstructed and need not be stored
    explicitly.)

6.  Deterministic update.  For every residue r set the affine map

          \Phi _r : F_q^{\,d} \longrightarrow  F_q^{\,d},                          (7)
          \Phi _r(u_0,\ldots ,u_{k-1})
              := ( u_1 , \ldots  , u_{k-1} ,
                   T_r(u_{a_1},\ldots ,u_{a_k}, u_1-u_0) ).

    With this definition one has

          S_{n+1} = \Phi _{\,n mod L}(S_n)  (n \geq  0).                   (8)

    Write \Phi _r(x)=B_r x+c_r with B_r\in End(F_q^{\,d}) and
    c_r\in F_q^{\,d}.                                                   (9)

7.  Linearisation of the affine maps.  
    Adjoin one dummy coordinate and set D:=d+1.  
    For every r define the D\times D matrix

          B_r :=
                [ B_r   c_r ]
                [ 0 \ldots  0  1  ]     \in  End(F_q^{\,D}).               (10)

    Composition of affine maps corresponds to multiplication of
    the B_r.

8.  Semigroup exponent.  
    For a square matrix M over F_q let

          per(M) := minimal positive t for which
                     M^{s+t}=M^{s}  for some s \geq  0.                 (11)

    For any finite \Sigma \subset End(F_q^{\,D}) put  

          exp \Sigma  := lcm{ per(M) : M\in \Sigma  }.                           (12)

    A crude universal estimate is  

          per(M) \leq  q^{D^2} (for every M),  
          exp \Sigma  \leq  (q^{D^2})! \leq  q^{D^2\cdot q^{D^2}}.                      (12')

    (12') will never be used later; it is supplied only for orientation.

Tasks.  

(A)  Prove that every coordinate difference sequence
     (\Delta _n^{(j)})_{n\geq 0} is eventually periodic.

(B)  Show that (S_n)_{n\geq 0} is eventually periodic and that one can
     choose a period P satisfying

          P  divides  L \cdot  p^{\lceil log_p D\rceil } \cdot  exp\langle B_0,\ldots ,B_{L-1}\rangle .   (13)

     where \langle B_0,\ldots ,B_{L-1}\rangle  denotes the semigroup
     generated by {B_0,\ldots ,B_{L-1}}.

(C)  Design an explicit deterministic algorithm which, given the initial
     block (2), outputs an onset index i and a period P obeying (13) and
     runs in time

          O( L \cdot  q^{\,D^2} \cdot  poly(m,k,log q) ).                    (14)
kernel_variant.solution:
Notation and preliminary lift.  
Write  

      S := F_q^{\,d},           S := {(x,1) : x\in S} \subset  F_q^{\,D}.   (15)

(The projection (x,1)\mapsto x is a bijection S\to S.)

For every residue r set the lifted linear map  

      \Phi _r := B_r \in  End(F_q^{\,D}).                            (16)

Thus for all n \geq  0  

      (S_{n+1},1)=\Phi _{\,n mod L}(S_n,1).                          (17)

Step 1.  Reduction to a fixed linear endomorphism.  
Define the ``principal'' L-step matrix  

      M := \Phi _{L-1} \cdot  \ldots  \cdot  \Phi _1 \cdot  \Phi _0  \in  End(F_q^{\,D}).         (18)

Because n\mapsto (S_n,1) is updated by \Phi _{r(n)} with r(n)=n mod L, one has  
for every integer t \geq  0

      (S_{(t+1)L},1)=M\cdot (S_{tL},1).                                (19)

Consequently the sub-sequence  

      Z_t := (S_{tL},1) (t=0,1,2,\ldots )                              (20)

evolves by the single linear rule Z_{t+1}=MZ_t.

Step 2.  Bounding the period of M.  
Let  

      \Sigma  := \langle \Phi _0,\ldots ,\Phi _{L-1}\rangle  = \langle B_0,\ldots ,B_{L-1}\rangle    (finite semigroup), (21)
      e   := exp \Sigma .                                               (22)

Because M is a word in the generators \Phi _r, we have M\in \Sigma  and therefore  

      per(M) | e.                                                 (23)

Put  

      h := \lceil log_p D\rceil  so p^{h} \geq  D.                              (24)

Multiplying the divisor (23) by the harmless factor p^{h} gives  

      per(M)  |  p^{h}\cdot e.                                         (25)

(The only reason for inserting p^{h} is to make the forthcoming global
period agree with the prescribed structure (13); it has no independent
mathematical necessity.)

Step 3.  Periodicity of the principal subsequence Z_t.  
Choose integers \sigma  \geq  0 and t := per(M) such that  

      M^{\sigma +t}=M^{\sigma }.                                              (26)

Then (19) implies  

      Z_{t+\sigma }=M^{t}Z_{\sigma }=Z_{\sigma },                                   (27)

so (Z_t)_{t\geq \sigma } is periodic with period t dividing p^{h}e.

Step 4.  Lifting the period to the full time line.  
Set  

      P := L \cdot  t;  then P | L\cdot p^{h}\cdot e.                            (28)

For n \geq  \sigma L write n = \tau L + r with 0\leq r<L.  Successively applying the
update rule (17) for the first r steps and (19) for the remaining \tau 
blocks of length L one obtains

      (S_{n+P},1)
      = \Phi _{r-1}\ldots \Phi _0 \cdot  M^{\tau +t} \cdot  (S_0,1)                        (29)
      = \Phi _{r-1}\ldots \Phi _0 \cdot  M^{\tau }     \cdot  (S_0,1)                      (30)
      = (S_n,1).                                                 (31)

Hence (S_n)_{n\geq \sigma L} is periodic with period P, and each difference
sequence \Delta ^{(j)}_n = x^{(j)}_{n+1}-x^{(j)}_n inherits this periodicity.
Tasks (A) and (B) are complete.

Step 5.  Constructive algorithm (Task C).  

(i)  Cycle finder.  
Apply Brent's tortoise-hare algorithm to the finite sequence
n\mapsto (S_n,1).  One evaluation of the update uses exactly one \Phi _r, and
each such evaluation costs \Theta (poly(m,k,log q)) field-bit operations.  
Because

      |S| = q^{d} = q^{D-1},                                     (32)

the first repetition appears after at most q^{d} steps.  Therefore the
search phase runs in

      O( q^{d} \cdot  poly(m,k,log q) ) \subset  O( L \cdot  q^{D^2} \cdot  poly(\ldots ) ).   (33)

(The inclusion follows from d\leq D\leq D^2 and L\geq 1.)

(ii)  Post-processing.  
Standard post-processing returns

      * i  = the first index on the eventual cycle,
      * Q = the minimal period of (S_n).                         (34)

(iii)  Compatibility with the theoretical bound.  
Because Q is minimal, it divides every other period of the sequence; in
particular

      Q | L\cdot t | L\cdot p^{h}\cdot e.                                        (35)

Thus we may output P:=Q, which satisfies (13).  The running-time bound
(33) is within the requirement (14), so Task (C) is settled. \blacksquare

original_kernel_variant.question:
(The statement of the enhanced problem was already mathematically sound; no
modifications are required.  It is reproduced here verbatim for
convenience.)

      L \geq  1   (external clock);

      0 = a_1 < a_2 < \cdots  < a_k \leq  k-1.                                   (\star )

1.  Local rules.  
    For every residue r\in {0,\ldots ,L-1} an affine map

          T_r : F_q^{\,m(k+1)}  \longrightarrow   F_q^{\,m},                     (1)

    the B_r.

          exp \Sigma  \leq  (q^{D^2})! \leq  q^{D^2\cdot q^{D^2}}.                      (12')

(A)  Prove that every coordinate difference sequence
     (\Delta _n^{(j)})_{n\geq 0} is eventually periodic.

          P  divides  L \cdot  p^{\lceil log_p D\rceil } \cdot  exp\langle B_0,\ldots ,B_{L-1}\rangle .   (13)

     block (2), outputs an onset index i and a period P obeying (13) and
     runs in time

          O( L \cdot  q^{\,D^2} \cdot  poly(m,k,log q) ).                    (14)

Notation and preliminary lift.  
Write  

      S := F_q^{\,d},           S := {(x,1) : x\in S} \subset  F_q^{\,D}.   (15)

(The projection (x,1)\mapsto x is a bijection S\to S.)

For every residue r set the lifted linear map  

      \Phi _r := B_r \in  End(F_q^{\,D}).                            (16)

Thus for all n \geq  0  

      (S_{n+1},1)=\Phi _{\,n mod L}(S_n,1).                          (17)

Define the ``principal'' L-step matrix  

      M := \Phi _{L-1} \cdot  \ldots  \cdot  \Phi _1 \cdot  \Phi _0  \in  End(F_q^{\,D}).         (18)

Because n\mapsto (S_n,1) is updated by \Phi _{r(n)} with r(n)=n mod L, one has  
for every integer t \geq  0

Consequently the sub-sequence  

      Z_t := (S_{tL},1) (t=0,1,2,\ldots )                              (20)

Step 2.  Bounding the period of M.  
Let  

      \Sigma  := \langle \Phi _0,\ldots ,\Phi _{L-1}\rangle  = \langle B_0,\ldots ,B_{L-1}\rangle    (finite semigroup), (21)
      e   := exp \Sigma .                                               (22)

period agree with the prescribed structure (13); it has no independent
mathematical necessity.)

      M^{\sigma +t}=M^{\sigma }.                                              (26)

Then (19) implies  

      Z_{t+\sigma }=M^{t}Z_{\sigma }=Z_{\sigma },                                   (27)

so (Z_t)_{t\geq \sigma } is periodic with period t dividing p^{h}e.

      P := L \cdot  t;  then P | L\cdot p^{h}\cdot e.                            (28)

For n \geq  \sigma L write n = \tau L + r with 0\leq r<L.  Successively applying the
update rule (17) for the first r steps and (19) for the remaining \tau 
blocks of length L one obtains

      (S_{n+P},1)
      = \Phi _{r-1}\ldots \Phi _0 \cdot  M^{\tau +t} \cdot  (S_0,1)                        (29)
      = \Phi _{r-1}\ldots \Phi _0 \cdot  M^{\tau }     \cdot  (S_0,1)                      (30)
      = (S_n,1).                                                 (31)

Hence (S_n)_{n\geq \sigma L} is periodic with period P, and each difference
sequence \Delta ^{(j)}_n = x^{(j)}_{n+1}-x^{(j)}_n inherits this periodicity.
Tasks (A) and (B) are complete.

Step 5.  Constructive algorithm (Task C).  

(i)  Cycle finder.  
Apply Brent's tortoise-hare algorithm to the finite sequence
n\mapsto (S_n,1).  One evaluation of the update uses exactly one \Phi _r, and
each such evaluation costs \Theta (poly(m,k,log q)) field-bit operations.  
Because

      |S| = q^{d} = q^{D-1},                                     (32)

the first repetition appears after at most q^{d} steps.  Therefore the
search phase runs in

      O( q^{d} \cdot  poly(m,k,log q) ) \subset  O( L \cdot  q^{D^2} \cdot  poly(\ldots ) ).   (33)

(The inclusion follows from d\leq D\leq D^2 and L\geq 1.)

(ii)  Post-processing.  
Standard post-processing returns

      * i  = the first index on the eventual cycle,
      * Q = the minimal period of (S_n).                         (34)

(iii)  Compatibility with the theoretical bound.  
Because Q is minimal, it divides every other period of the sequence; in
particular

      Q | L\cdot t | L\cdot p^{h}\cdot e.                                        (35)

Thus we may output P:=Q, which satisfies (13).  The running-time bound
(33) is within the requirement (14), so Task (C) is settled. \blacksquare
